right_onstr | Expr | Sequence [ str | Expr ] | Nonedefault: None

Name(s) of the columns to join on from the rightTableFrame. It must be used together wit the left_on parameter. It cannot be used with the on parameter.

suffixstrdefault: '_right'

Duplicate columns on the rightTable are appended this suffix.

join_nullsbooldefault: False

Ifnull value matches should produce join rows or not.

coalescebool | Nonedefault: None

Collapse join columns into a single column.

Example​

>>> import tabsdata as td
>>>
>>> tf1: td.TableFrame ...
>>>
┌──────┬──────┐
│ a    ┆ b    │
│ ---  ┆ ---  │
│ str  ┆ i64  │
╞══════╪══════╡
│ A    ┆ 1    │
│ X    ┆ 10   │
│ C    ┆ 3    │
│ D    ┆ 5    │
│ M    ┆ 9    │
│ A    ┆ 100  │
│ M    ┆ 50   │
│ null ┆ 20   │
│ F    ┆ null │
└──────┴──────┘
>>>
>>> tf2: td.TableFrame ...
>>>
┌──────┬──────┐
│ a    ┆ b    │
│ ---  ┆ ---  │
│ str  ┆ i64  │
╞══════╪══════╡
│ A    ┆ 3    │
│ Y    ┆ 4    │
│ Z    ┆ 5    │
│ A    ┆ 0    │
│ M    ┆ 6    │
│ null ┆ 8    │
│ F    ┆ null │
└──────┴──────┘
>>>
An inner join:
>>>
>>> tf1.join(tf2, on="a", how="inner")
>>>
┌─────┬──────┬─────────┐
│ a   ┆ b    ┆ b_right │
│ --- ┆ ---  ┆ ---     │
│ str ┆ i64  ┆ i64     │
╞═════╪══════╪═════════╡
│ A   ┆ 1    ┆ 3       │
│ A   ┆ 1    ┆ 0       │
│ M   ┆ 9    ┆ 6       │
│ A   ┆ 100  ┆ 3       │
│ A   ┆ 100  ┆ 0       │
│ M   ┆ 50   ┆ 6       │
│ F   ┆ null ┆ null    │
└─────┴──────┴─────────┘
>>>
A left join:
>>>
>>> tf1.join(tf2, on="a", how="left")
>>>
┌──────┬──────┬─────────┐
│ a    ┆ b    ┆ b_right │
│ ---  ┆ ---  ┆ ---     │
│ str  ┆ i64  ┆ i64     │
╞══════╪══════╪═════════╡
│ A    ┆ 1    ┆ 3       │
│ A    ┆ 1    ┆ 0       │
│ X    ┆ 10   ┆ null    │
│ C    ┆ 3    ┆ null    │
│ D    ┆ 5    ┆ null    │
│ …    ┆ …    ┆ …       │
│ A    ┆ 100  ┆ 3       │
│ A    ┆ 100  ┆ 0       │
│ M    ┆ 50   ┆ 6       │
│ null ┆ 20   ┆ null    │
│ F    ┆ null ┆ null    │
└──────┴──────┴─────────┘
>>>
Turning off column coalesce:
>>>
>>> tf1.join(tf2, on="a", coalesce=False)
>>>
┌─────┬──────┬─────────┬─────────┐
│ a   ┆ b    ┆ a_right ┆ b_right │
│ --- ┆ ---  ┆ ---     ┆ ---     │
│ str ┆ i64  ┆ str     ┆ i64     │
╞═════╪══════╪═════════╪═════════╡
│ A   ┆ 1    ┆ A       ┆ 3       │
│ A   ┆ 1    ┆ A       ┆ 0       │
│ M   ┆ 9    ┆ M       ┆ 6       │
│ A   ┆ 100  ┆ A       ┆ 3       │
│ A   ┆ 100  ┆ A       ┆ 0       │
│ M   ┆ 50   ┆ M       ┆ 6       │
│ F   ┆ null ┆ F       ┆ null    │
└─────┴──────┴─────────┴─────────┘
last
def last() -> TableFrame

Return a TableFrame with the last row.

Categories: filters

Example​

>>> import tabsdata as td
>>>
>>> tf: td.TableFrame ...
>>>
┌─────┬─────┐
│ a   ┆ b   │
│ --- ┆ --- │
│ str ┆ i64 │
╞═════╪═════╡
│ A   ┆ 1   │
│ X   ┆ 10  │
│ C   ┆ 3   │
│ D   ┆ 5   │
│ M   ┆ 9   │
└─────┴─────┘
>>>
>>> tf.last()
>>>
┌─────┬─────┐
│ a   ┆ b   │
│ --- ┆ --- │
│ str ┆ i64 │
╞═════╪═════╡
│ M   ┆ 9   │
└─────┴─────┘
last_row
def last_row(
    named: bool = False,
) -> tuple[Any, ...] | dict[str, Any] | None

Return a tuple or dictionary with the last row, or None if no row.

Categories: filters

Example​

>>> import tabsdata as td
>>>
>>> tf: td.TableFrame ...
>>>
┌─────┬─────┐
│ A   ┆ B   │
│ --- ┆ --- │
│ str ┆ i64 │
╞═════╪═════╡
│ a   ┆ 1   │
│ b   ┆ 2   │
│ c   ┆ 3   │
└─────┴─────┘
>>>
>>> tf.last_row()
>>>
('c', 3)
>>>
>>> tf.last_row(named=True)
>>>
{'A': 'c', 'B': 3}
limit
def limit(
    n: int = 5,
) -> TableFrame

Return a TableFrame with the first n rows. This is equivalent to head.

Categories: filters

Parameters:

nintdefault: 5

The number of rows to return.

Example​

>>> import tabsdata as td
>>>
>>> tf: td.TableFrame ...
>>>
┌─────┬─────┐
│ a   ┆ b   │
│ --- ┆ --- │
│ str ┆ i64 │
╞═════╪═════╡
│ A   ┆ 1   │
│ X   ┆ 10  │
│ C   ┆ 3   │
│ D   ┆ 5   │
│ M   ┆ 9   │
└─────┴─────┘
>>>
>>> tf.limit(2)
>>>
┌─────┬─────┐
│ a   ┆ b   │
│ --- ┆ --- │
│ str ┆ i64 │
╞═════╪═════╡
│ A   ┆ 1   │
│ X   ┆ 10  │
└─────┴─────┘
rename
def rename(
    mapping: dict [ str , str ],
) -> TableFrame

Rename columns from the TableFrame.

Categories: projection

Parameters:

mappingdict [ str , str ]required

A dictionary mapping column names to their new names. The operation will fail if any specified column name does not exist.

Examples​

>>> import tabsdata as td
>>>
>>> tf: td.TableFrame ...
>>>
┌──────┬──────┐
│ i    ┆ f    │
│ ---  ┆ ---  │
│ i32  ┆ f64  │
╞══════╪══════╡
│ 1    ┆ 3.1  │
│ 2    ┆ 4.1  │
│ 3    ┆ 5.9  │
│ 4    ┆ 2.6  │
│ 5    ┆ 53.5 │
│ 6    ┆ 8.97 │
└──────┴──────┘
>>>
>>> tf.{"i": "index", "f": "amount"})
>>>
┌───────┬────────┐
│ index ┆ amount │
│ ----- ┆ ------ │
│ i32   ┆ f64    │
╞═══════╪════════╡
│ 1     ┆ 3.1    │
│ 2     ┆ 4.1    │
│ 3     ┆ 5.9    │
│ 4     ┆ 2.6    │
│ 5     ┆ 53.5   │
│ 6     ┆ 8.97   │
└───────┴────────┘
select
def select(
    * exprs: td_typing.IntoExpr | Iterable [ td_typing.IntoExpr ],
    ** named_exprs: td_typing.IntoExpr,
) -> TableFrame

Select column(s) from the TableFrame.

Categories: projection

Parameters:

exprsrequired

Columns or expressions to select.

named_exprsrequired

Named expressions to select.

Example​

>>> import tabsdata as td
>>>
>>> tf: td.TableFrame ...
>>>
┌─────┬─────┐
│ a   ┆ b   │
│ --- ┆ --- │
│ str ┆ i64 │
╞═════╪═════╡
│ A   ┆ 1   │
│ X   ┆ 10  │
│ C   ┆ 3   │
│ D   ┆ 5   │
│ M   ┆ 9   │
│ A   ┆ 100 │
│ M   ┆ 50  │
└─────┴─────┘
>>>
>>> tf.select(td.col("a"), td.col("b").mul(2).alias("bx2"),)
>>>
┌─────┬─────┐
│ a   ┆ bx2 │
│ --- ┆ --- │
│ str ┆ i64 │
╞═════╪═════╡
│ A   ┆ 2   │
│ X   ┆ 20  │
│ C   ┆ 6   │
│ D   ┆ 10  │
│ M   ┆ 18  │
│ A   ┆ 200 │
│ M   ┆ 100 │
└─────┴─────┘
slice
def slice(
    offset: int,
    length: int | None = None,
) -> TableFrame

Return a TableFrame with a slice of the original TableFrame

Categories: filters

Parameters:

offsetintrequired

Slice starting index.

lengthint | Nonedefault: None

The length of the slice.None means all the way to the end.

Example​

>>> import tabsdata as td
>>>
>>> tf: td.TableFrame ...
>>>
┌─────┬─────┐
│ a   ┆ b   │
│ --- ┆ --- │
│ str ┆ i64 │
╞═════╪═════╡
│ A   ┆ 1   │
│ X   ┆ 10  │
│ C   ┆ 3   │
│ D   ┆ 5   │
│ M   ┆ 9   │
└─────┴─────┘
>>>
>>> tf.slice(2,2)
>>>
┌─────┬─────┐
│ a   ┆ b   │
│ --- ┆ --- │
│ str ┆ i64 │
╞═════╪═════╡
│ C   ┆ 3   │
│ D   ┆ 5   │
└─────┴─────┘
sort
def sort(
    by: td_typing.IntoExpr | Iterable [ td_typing.IntoExpr ],
    * more_by: td_typing.IntoExpr,
    descending: bool | Sequence [ bool ] = False,
    nulls_last: bool | Sequence [ bool ] = False,
    maintain_order: bool = False,
) -> TableFrame

Sort the TableFrame by the given column(s) or expression(s).

Categories: tableframe

Parameters:

bytd_typing.IntoExpr | Iterable [ td_typing.IntoExpr ]required

Column(s) or expression(s) to sort by.

more_byrequired

Additional colums to sort by.

descendingbool | Sequence [ bool ]default: False

Specifies if the sorting should be descending or not.

nulls_lastbool | Sequence [ bool ]default: False

Specifies ifnull values should be placed last.

maintain_orderbooldefault: False

Preserve the order of equal rows.

Example​

>>> import tabsdata as td
>>>
>>> tf: td.TableFrame ...
>>>
┌──────┬──────┐
│ a    ┆ b    │
│ ---  ┆ ---  │
│ str  ┆ i64  │
╞══════╪══════╡
│ A    ┆ 1    │
│ X    ┆ 10   │
│ C    ┆ 3    │
│ D    ┆ 5    │
│ M    ┆ 9    │
│ A    ┆ 100  │
│ M    ┆ 50   │
│ null ┆ 20   │
│ F    ┆ null │
└──────┴──────┘
>>>
>>> tf.sort(td.col("a"), descending = True)
>>>
┌──────┬───────┐
│ a    ┆ b     │
│ ---  ┆ ---   │
│ str  ┆ f32   │
╞══════╪═══════╡
│ A    ┆ 1.0   │
│ X    ┆ 10.0  │
│ C    ┆ 3.0   │
│ D    ┆ 5.0   │
│ M    ┆ 9.0   │
│ A    ┆ 100.0 │
│ M    ┆ 50.0  │
│ null ┆ 20.0  │
│ F    ┆ null  │
└──────┴───────┘
tail
def tail(
    n: int = 5,
) -> TableFrame

Return a TableFrame with the last n rows.

Categories: filters

Parameters:

nintdefault: 5

The number of rows to return.

Example​

>>> import tabsdata as td
>>>
>>> tf: td.TableFrame ...
>>>
┌─────┬─────┐
│ a   ┆ b   │
│ --- ┆ --- │
│ str ┆ i64 │
╞═════╪═════╡
│ A   ┆ 1   │
│ X   ┆ 10  │
│ C   ┆ 3   │
│ D   ┆ 5   │
│ M   ┆ 9   │
└─────┴─────┘
>>>
>>> tf.tail(2)
>>>
┌─────┬─────┐
│ a   ┆ b   │
│ --- ┆ --- │
│ str ┆ i64 │
╞═════╪═════╡
│ D   ┆ 5   │
│ M   ┆ 9   │
└─────┴─────┘
to_dict
def to_dict() -> dict[str, list[Any]]

Creates a dictionary from this tableframe.

Categories: tableframe

to_pandas
def to_pandas() -> pd.DataFrame

Creates a pandas dataframe from this tableframe.

Categories: tableframe

to_polars_df
def to_polars_df() -> DataFrame

Creates a polars dataframe from this tableframe.

Categories: tableframe

to_polars_lf
def to_polars_lf() -> LazyFrame

Creates a polars lazyframe from this tableframe.

Categories: tableframe

udf
def udf(
    on: td_typing.IntoExpr | list [ td_typing.IntoExpr ],
    function: td_udf.UDF,
) -> TableFrame

Apply a user-defined function (UDF) to the columns resolved by expr.

The selected columns are supplied to function, which can implement either on_batch or on_element. An on_batch implementation receives a list of Polars series representing the selected columns and must return a list of Polars series with matching length. An on_element implementation receives a list of Python scalars for each row and returns a list of scalars; the framework wraps this in an efficient batch executor, so data still flows in batches even when authoring row-wise logic. In both cases the returned series become new columns appended to the original TableFrame.

By default, both methods receive their inputs as a list. Override the signature property in your UDF class to return “unpacked” to have each column passed as a separate argument instead.

Using UDFs:

Categories: projection

Parameters:

ontd_typing.IntoExpr | list [ td_typing.IntoExpr ]required

Expression selecting the input column(s) of the UDF.

functiontd_udf.UDFrequired

Instance oftabsdata.tableframe.udf.function.UDF defining on_batch or on_element to produce the output series.

Examples​

>>> import tabsdata as td
>>> import tabsdata.tableframe as tdf
>>>
>>> class SumUDF(tdf.UDF):
...     def __init__(self):
...         super().__init__(("total", tdf.Int64))
...
...     def on_batch(self, series):
...         return [series[0] + series[1]]
>>>
>>> tf = td.TableFrame({"a": [1, 2, 3], "b": [10, 20, 30]})
>>> print(tf)
┌─────┬─────┐
│ a   ┆ b   │
│ --- ┆ --- │
│ i64 ┆ i64 │
╞═════╪═════╡
│ 1   ┆ 10  │
│ 2   ┆ 20  │
│ 3   ┆ 30  │
└─────┴─────┘
>>> tf.udf(td.col("a", "b"), SumUDF())
>>> print(tf)
┌─────┬─────┬───────┐
│ a   ┆ b   ┆ total │
│ --- ┆ --- ┆ ---   │
│ i64 ┆ i64 ┆ i64   │
╞═════╪═════╪═══════╡
│ 1   ┆ 10  ┆ 11    │
│ 2   ┆ 20  ┆ 22    │
│ 3   ┆ 30  ┆ 33    │
└─────┴─────┴───────┘
>>> class RatioUDF(tdf.UDF):
...     def __init__(self):
...         super().__init__(("ratio", tdf.Float64))
...
...     def on_element(self, values):
...         return [values[0] / values[1]]
>>>
>>> tf = td.TableFrame({"numerator": [10, 20, 30],
>>>                     "denominator": [2, 5, 10],})
>>> print(tf)
┌───────────┬──────────────┐
│ numerator ┆ denominator  │
│ ---       ┆ ---          │
│ i64       ┆ i64          │
╞═══════════╪══════════════╡
│ 10        ┆ 2            │
│ 20        ┆ 5            │
│ 30        ┆ 10           │
└───────────┴──────────────┘
>>> tf.udf(td.col("numerator", "denominator"), RatioUDF()).collect()
>>> print(tf)
┌───────────┬──────────────┬──────┐
│ numerator ┆ denominator  ┆ ratio│
│ ---       ┆ ---          ┆ ---  │
│ i64       ┆ i64          ┆ f64  │
╞═══════════╪══════════════╪══════╡
│ 10        ┆ 2            ┆ 5.0  │
│ 20        ┆ 5            ┆ 4.0  │
│ 30        ┆ 10           ┆ 3.0  │
└───────────┴──────────────┴──────┘
>>> class RatioUnpackedUDF(tdf.UDF):
...     def __init__(self):
...         super().__init__(("ratio", tdf.Float64))
...
...     @property
...     def signature(self):
...         return "unpacked"
...
...     def on_element(self, numerator, denominator):
...         return [numerator / denominator]
>>>
>>> tf = td.TableFrame({"numerator": [10, 20, 30],
>>>                     "denominator": [2, 5, 10],})
>>> tf.udf(td.col("numerator", "denominator"), RatioUnpackedUDF()).collect()
>>> print(tf)
┌───────────┬──────────────┬──────┐
│ numerator ┆ denominator  ┆ ratio│
│ ---       ┆ ---          ┆ ---  │
│ i64       ┆ i64          ┆ f64  │
╞═══════════╪══════════════╪══════╡
│ 10        ┆ 2            ┆ 5.0  │
│ 20        ┆ 5            ┆ 4.0  │
│ 30        ┆ 10           ┆ 3.0  │
└───────────┴──────────────┴──────┘
unique
def unique(
    subset: td_typing.ColumnNameOrSelector | Collection [ td_typing.ColumnNameOrSelector ] | None = None,
    *,
    keep: td_typing.UniqueKeepStrategy = 'any',
    maintain_order: bool = False,
) -> TableFrame

Deduplicate rows from the TableFrame.

Categories: filters

Parameters:

subsettd_typing.ColumnNameOrSelector | Collection [ td_typing.ColumnNameOrSelector ] | Nonedefault: None

Columns to evaluate for duplicate values. If None, all columns are considered.

keeptd_typing.UniqueKeepStrategydefault: 'any'

Strategy to keep duplicates:first, last, any, none ( eliminate duplicate rows).

maintain_orderbooldefault: False

Preserve the order of the rows.

Example​

>>> import tabsdata as td
>>>
>>> tf: td.TableFrame ...
>>>
┌──────┬──────┐
│ a    ┆ b    │
│ ---  ┆ ---  │
│ str  ┆ i64  │
╞══════╪══════╡
│ A    ┆ 1    │
│ X    ┆ 10   │
│ C    ┆ 3    │
│ D    ┆ 5    │
│ M    ┆ 9    │
│ A    ┆ 100  │
│ M    ┆ 50   │
│ null ┆ 20   │
│ F    ┆ null │
└──────┴──────┘
>>>
>>> tf.unique("a", keep="last")
>>>
┌──────┬──────┐
│ a    ┆ b    │
│ ---  ┆ ---  │
│ str  ┆ i64  │
╞══════╪══════╡
│ D    ┆ 5    │
│ C    ┆ 3    │
│ X    ┆ 10   │
│ A    ┆ 100  │
│ M    ┆ 50   │
│ F    ┆ null │
│ null ┆ 20   │
└──────┴──────┘
unnest
def unnest(
    columns: td_typing.ColumnNameOrSelector | Collection [ td_typing.ColumnNameOrSelector ],
    * more_columns: td_typing.ColumnNameOrSelector,
) -> TableFrame

Expand one or more struct columns so that each field within the struct becomes a separate column in the TableFrame.

The resulting TableFrame will place these new columns in the same position as the original struct column, effectively replacing it. This makes it easier to work directly with the inner fields as standard columns.

Categories: projection

Parameters:

columnstd_typing.ColumnNameOrSelector | Collection [ td_typing.ColumnNameOrSelector ]required

Name of the struct column(s) to expand.

more_columnsrequired

Additional struct columns to expand, provided as positional arguments.

Example​

>>> import tabsdata as td
>>>
>>> tf = td.TableFrame({
...     "id": [1, 2],
...     "info": [
...         {"name": "Alice", "age": 30},
...         {"name": "Bob", "age": None},
...     ],
...     "status": ["active", "inactive"],
... })
>>>
>>> tf
>>>
┌─────┬───────────────┬───────────┐
│ id  ┆ info          ┆ status    │
│ --- ┆ ---           ┆ ---       │
│ i64 ┆ struct[2]     ┆ str       │
╞═════╪═══════════════╪═══════════╡
│ 1   ┆ {"Alice",30}  ┆ active    │
│ 2   ┆ {"Bob",null}  ┆ inactive  │
└─────┴───────────────┴───────────┘
>>>
>>> tf.unnest("info")
>>>
┌─────┬───────┬──────┬───────────┐
│ id  ┆ name  ┆ age  ┆ status    │
│ --- ┆ ---   ┆ ---  ┆ ---       │
│ i64 ┆ str   ┆ i64  ┆ str       │
╞═════╪═══════╪══════╪═══════════╡
│ 1   ┆ Alice ┆ 30   ┆ active    │
│ 2   ┆ Bob   ┆ null ┆ inactive  │
└─────┴───────┴──────┴───────────┘
with_columns
def with_columns(
    * exprs: td_typing.IntoExpr | Iterable [ td_typing.IntoExpr ],
    ** named_exprs: td_typing.IntoExpr,
) -> TableFrame

Add columns to the TableFrame.

Categories: projection

Parameters:

exprsrequired

Columns or expressions to add.

named_exprsrequired

Named expressions to add.

Example​

>>> import tabsdata as td
>>>
>>> tf: td.TableFrame ...
>>>
┌──────┬──────┐
│ x    ┆ y    │
│ ---  ┆ ---  │
│ f64  ┆ f64  │
╞══════╪══════╡
│ 1.0  ┆ 2.0  │
│ 2.0  ┆ 2.0  │
│ 10.0 ┆ 10.0 │
│ 4.0  ┆ 10.0 │
│ 5.0  ┆ null │
│ null ┆ null │
└──────┴──────┘
>>>
>>> tf.with_columns(td.col("x").mul(td.col("y")).alias("z"))
>>>
┌──────┬──────┬──────┐
│ x    ┆ y    ┆ z    │
│ ---  ┆ ---  ┆ ---  │
│ f64  ┆ f64  ┆ f64  │
╞══════╪══════╪══════╡
│ 1.0  ┆ 2.0  ┆ 2.0  │
│ 2.0  ┆ 2.0  ┆ 4.0  │
│ NaN  ┆ NaN  ┆ NaN  │
│ 4.0  ┆ NaN  ┆ NaN  │
│ 5.0  ┆ null ┆ null │
│ null ┆ null ┆ null │
└──────┴──────┴──────┘
TableInput
class TableInput(
    table: str | List [ str ],
)

Bases: SourcePlugin

Table-based data inputs.

stream​

def stream(
    working_dir: str,
) -> list[TableFrame | None | list[TableFrame | None]]
tablestr | List[str]

The table(s) to load.

Type: str | List[ str ]

stream
def stream(
    working_dir: str,
) -> list[TableFrame | None | list[TableFrame | None]]
TableOutput
class TableOutput(
    table: str | list [ str ],
)

Bases: DestinationPlugin

Table-based data outputs.

Categories: destination

tablestr | list[str]

The table(s) to create. If multiple tables are provided, they must be provided as a list.

Type: str | list [ str ]

TabsdataFunction
class TabsdataFunction(
    func: Callable,
    name: str | None,
    input: SourcePlugin = None,
    output: DestinationPlugin = None,
    trigger_by: str | list [ str ] | CronTrigger | StageTrigger | None = None,
    output_actions: DataQuality | list [ DataQuality ] | None = None,
)

Bases: object

Attributes:

inputSourcePlugin | None

The data to be used when running the function.

Type: SourcePlugin | None

namestr

The name with which the function will be registered.

Type: str

original_filestr

The file where the original function is defined in the user’s computer

original_folderstr

The folder where the original function is defined, as a local path in the user’s computer.

Type: str

original_functionCallable

The original function that was decorated, without any behaviour modifications.

Type: Callable

outputDestinationPlugin | None

The location where the function results will be saved when run.

Type: DestinationPlugin

output_actionslist[OnTablesAction]

Actions to perform on the output tables.

Type: list [OnTablesAction]

trigger_bylist[str]| CronTrigger | StageTrigger | None

The trigger(s) that will cause the function to execute. It must be another table or tables in the system, a CronTrigger, or a StageTrigger in the case of a publisher.

Type: List[ str ]

typestr

The type of the function. It can be “publisher”, “transformer” or “subscriber”.

Type: str

UserPasswordCredentials
class UserPasswordCredentials(
    user: str | Secret,
    password: str | Secret,
)

Bases: Credentials

Represents credentials consisting of a user and a password.

This class is used to manage and securely handle user and password credentials. It includes functionalities to set user and password details, convert them into a dictionary representation, and provide a string representation for debugging purposes.

Categories: credentials

passwordSecret

The password.

Type: Secret

userSecret

The user.

Type: Secret

concat
def concat(
    items: Iterable [ TdType ] | None,
    how: Literal [ 'vertical' , 'vertical_relaxed' , 'diagonal' , 'diagonal_relaxed' ] = 'vertical',
) -> TdType | None

Combine multiple TableFrames by stacking their rows.

Categories: union

Parameters:

itemsIterable [ TdType ] | Nonerequired

The TableFrames to concatenate. If None or empty, returns None. None entries within the iterable are ignored. If all entries are None, returns None.

howLiteral [ 'vertical' , 'vertical_relaxed' , 'diagonal' , 'diagonal_relaxed' ]default: 'vertical'

{‘vertical’, ‘vertical_relaxed’, ‘diagonal’, ‘diagonal_relaxed’} vertical: Appends the rows of each input below the previous one. All inputs must have exactly the same column names and types; otherwise the operation fails. vertical_relaxed: Same as vertical, but if columns with the same name have different data types across inputs, they are converted to a common type (e.g. Int32 → Int64). diagonal: Aligns columns by name across all inputs. If a column is missing from a particular input, that input is padded with null values for the missing column. Matching columns keep their original type if consistent. diagonal_relaxed: Same as diagonal, but when matching columns have different data types, they are converted to a common type (e.g. Int32 → Int64).

Example​

>>> import tabsdata as td
>>>
>>> tf1: td.TableFrame ...
>>>
┌──────┬──────┐
│ a    ┆ b    │
│ ---  ┆ ---  │
│ str  ┆ i64  │
╞══════╪══════╡
│ a    ┆ 1    │
│ b    ┆ 2    │
└──────┴──────┘
>>>
>>> tf2: td.TableFrame ...
>>>
┌──────┬──────┐
│ a    ┆ b    │
│ ---  ┆ ---  │
│ str  ┆ i64  │
╞══════╪══════╡
│ x    ┆ 10   │
│ y    ┆ 20   │
└──────┴──────┘
>>>
>>> tf = td.concat([tf1, tf2])
>>>
┌──────┬──────┐
│ a    ┆ b    │
│ ---  ┆ ---  │
│ str  ┆ i64  │
╞══════╪══════╡
│ a    ┆ 1    │
│ b    ┆ 2    │
│ x    ┆ 10   │
│ y    ┆ 20   │
└──────┴──────┘
publisher
def publisher(
    source: AzureSource | LocalFileSource | MariaDBSource | MySQLSource | OracleSource | PostgresSource | S3Source | SourcePlugin,
    tables: TableOutput | str | List [ str ],
    name: str = None,
    trigger_by: PublisherTriggerBySpec = None,
    on_tables: OnTablesSpec = None,
) -> callable

Decorator to define a function to publish data to Tabsdata.

Categories: publisher

Parameters:

sourceAzureSource | LocalFileSource | MariaDBSource | MySQLSource | OracleSource | PostgresSource | S3Source | SourcePluginrequired

Where to obtain the data that will be provided as an input to the function.

tablesTableOutput | str | List [ str ]required

Where to store the output of the function.

namestrdefault: None

The name with which the function will be registered. If not provided, the current function name will be used.

trigger_byPublisherTriggerBySpecdefault: None

The trigger that will cause the function to execute. It can be a table in the system, a list of tables, None (in which case it must be triggered manually), or a CronTrigger.

on_tablesOnTablesSpecdefault: None

Actions to perform on output tables after executing the function. Can be a single action or a list of them. Defaults to None.

Returns: The function converted to a Tabsdata Function.

Return type: callable

subscriber
def subscriber(
    tables: TableInput | str | List [ str ],
    destination: AzureDestination | LocalFileDestination | MariaDBDestination | MySQLDestination | OracleDestination | PostgresDestination | S3Destination | DestinationPlugin,
    name: str = None,
    trigger_by: SubscriberTriggerBySpec = '*',
) -> callable

Decorator to define a function to subscribe to data present in Tabsdata.

Categories: subscriber

Parameters:

tablesTableInput | str | List [ str ]required

Where to obtain the data that will be provided as an input to the function.

destinationAzureDestination | LocalFileDestination | MariaDBDestination | MySQLDestination | OracleDestination | PostgresDestination | S3Destination | DestinationPluginrequired

Where to store the output of the function.

namestrdefault: None

The name with which the function will be registered. If not provided, the current function name will be used.

trigger_bySubscriberTriggerBySpecdefault: '*'

The trigger that will cause the function to execute. It can be a table in the system, a list of tables, None (in which case it must be triggered manually), or a CronTrigger. Defaults to all dependencies.

Returns: The function converted to a Tabsdata Function.

Return type: callable

transformer
def transformer(
    input_tables: TableInput | str | List [ str ],
    output_tables: TableOutput | str | List [ str ],
    name: str = None,
    trigger_by: TransformerTriggerBySpec = '*',
    on_output_tables: OnTablesSpec = None,
) -> callable

Decorator to define a function to transform data present in Tabsdata.

Categories: transformer

Parameters:

input_tablesTableInput | str | List [ str ]required

Where to obtain the data that will be provided as an input to the function.

output_tablesTableOutput | str | List [ str ]required

Where to store the output of the function.

namestrdefault: None

The name with which the function will be registered. If not provided, the current function name will be used.

trigger_byTransformerTriggerBySpecdefault: '*'

The trigger that will cause the function to execute. It can be a table in the system, a list of tables, None (in which case it must be triggered manually), or a CronTrigger. Defaults to all dependencies.

on_output_tablesOnTablesSpecdefault: None

Actions to perform on output tables after executing the function. Can be a single action or a list of them. Defaults to None.

Returns: The function converted to a Tabsdata Function.

Return type: callable
